body_txt: For the Trope Namer website Superdickery.com, go here . It's a widespread tactic in serial fiction: show a teaser portraying the normally upright hero acting in an evil and despicable manner , causing the audience to wonder what the heck's going on and then, hopefully, to read/watch/listen to the thing you're advertising. You look at the cover to the newest issue of your favorite comic, and what do you see? The superhero , apparently killing their sidekick and Love Interest ! Or, On the Next episode of your favorite prime-time TV series, the main character goes bad , selling their team out to the Big Bad and shooting the Plucky Comic Relief in the face! So you read or watch the installment in question, and find out It Makes Sense in Context . The hero, or one of their allies, was The Mole , Not Himself , Really Not Himself , under possession or Mind Control , taking part in a Secret Test of Character , or had learned that if the Happily Adopted son had gotten what he wanted for Christmas, it would have resulted in the destruction of the entire universe. It is also entirely possible that it was All Just a Dream or a Show Within a Show . And many comic books flat-out ignore elements on the cover. For tv and movies, Manipulative Editing might also splice together two unrelated scenes for the promo, and it turns out the hero was being a dick to someone who actually deserved it. You should have known that Covers Always Lie and you can Never Trust a Trailer , but you were pulled in...by Superdickery! . Warning: Silver Age comics did have a tendency to induce Comedic Sociopathy in characters, alongside the strange plot devices and twists, as well 1950s Values Dissonance to modern readers. This means that even if Superman wasn't as evil as the cover made him look, the reader might still say "what a dick!" by the end of the story. The reason for this trope, however, isn't the writer's intent. During the Silver Age, the covers were designed first, and the writers had to work around that cover that had been drawn without a story. This trope doesn't really work with Anti-Heroes , as you generally expect such characters to be dicks. If the characterization sticks and/or gets worse, you may have Beware the Superman . In comics, a Cover Drop will often explain the truth behind the dickery. Compare with Clickbait Gag , an In-Universe use of one of Superdickery's web equivalents, Clickbait. No relation to Super Sex Organs .
